items.caption:
Most camp invites are NOT what parents think they are.

As a former Division 1 coach, scout, and recruiter, I can tell you this:

Just because your athlete got invited to a camp does NOT automatically mean they’re being heavily recruited.

A lot of these emails are mass blasts.

That’s why parents get frustrated…

They spend thousands on camps, hotels, flights, and showcases hoping for traction… and nothing happens.

Before committing to ANY camp, ask these 2 questions:

1️⃣ “Are you recruiting my position in my grad class?”
2️⃣ “Can you give feedback on my video?”

That second question is huge.

Because coaches who are genuinely interested usually engage.
Coaches who aren’t… usually don’t reply.

That alone can save families a ton of wasted time and money.

Then filter everything through:
✔️ Athletic fit
✔️ Academic fit
✔️ Geographic fit
✔️ Financial fit
✔️ Actual recruiting need

The families that win in recruiting usually aren’t attending the MOST events…

They’re attending the RIGHT ones.
items.transcript: I know decoding camp invites from schools and coaches could be a lot. You get a million of them and it's hard to filter through and hard to know what's legit, what's not. And if you're going to invest in a camp and go and travel to a camp, you want to make sure that it's worth it and that there's real opportunity there. And I was just talking to a parent, and hopefully this helps you, and we were filtering down their camps to make sure that they're going to the right ones. And a couple things to think about. One, reply back to the coach asking if they're in need of your position in your grad class, because not every school's in need of every position each year. So you want to make sure that they're in need of your position in your grad class. If you have a highlight video, include that in your email, preferably a YouTube link so they can click on it and it's easy for them to watch and it needs to be short as well. They don't want long highlight videos. And ask for feedback. If you don't get a reply, there's your answer right there, right? You don't need to go to the camp. It was probably a blast email. Other things to think about. Athletic standards. What are the athletic standards of that school? Does your athlete or do you come close to those athletic standards or projecting out to be? And it depends on your grade, right? If you're older, you need to be a little bit closer. If you're younger, not as much, but projecting out. And then academic standards, of course, can get into the school. You're close to the academic standards. Geographically, I would recommend staying somewhat local so it's easy for the college coaches to come watch you at your high school games or travel from there. Good luck.

items.caption:
Most families are chasing logos…
Smart families are chasing FIT.

Because the “best” school for your athlete isn’t just about athletics. It’s about:

→ Academics
→ Campus environment
→ Opportunity
→ Development
→ Network
→ Student experience
→ And finding a place where they can actually thrive for 4 years.

As a former Division 1 coach, scout, and recruiter, I can tell you this:

Too many athletes waste years targeting schools that were never the right fit in the first place.

The families that win in recruiting usually aren’t the ones hoping to get discovered…

They’re the ones with a real strategy.

Georgia has some unbelievable universities that can open doors athletically AND academically if you know how to position yourself the right way.

Which school would your athlete choose? 👇

Comment the school name below and I’ll tell you:
• What level athlete typically gets recruited there
• What coaches look for
• And how realistic that fit is right now

Save this for later because choosing the right fit can change your athlete’s entire future.

A lot of families are doing all the things…

Tournaments.
Showcases.
Camps.
Emails.
Highlight videos.

But there’s still no traction.

Here’s why:

College coaches usually do not walk into events blindly hoping to discover players.

They already have names they want to see.

So if your athlete is not being positioned before the event, there’s a good chance they are just hoping to get noticed.

And hope is not a recruiting strategy.

The goal is to target the right schools, understand who actually needs your athlete’s position, and get in front of those coaches before they show up.

That’s how you flip the script.

You stop waiting to be recruited.

You start recruiting the schools.

If you want a better plan to help your athlete get seen by the right college coaches, comment NATION and I’ll send you the playbook.
items.transcript: As a parent, I know it can be super frustrating helping your athlete go through this recruiting process, and there's nothing really happening. There's no traction, and you're doing all the things, and I know there's a million different opinions out there, and you're doing all the tournaments, showcases, and camps. But again, there's nothing happening. It's really out of like a hope, right? Hopefully somebody will discover them. Hopefully a school will discover my athlete. And you know that hope is not a good strategy going through this, really through anything. It's where you're proactive, right? You are going after. You're flipping the script. You are recruiting the schools, and you're going after them because these college coaches, as a former D1 coach, scout, and recruiter, these coaches have a list of athletes that are going to see at their tournaments or camps or showcases, and you got to get on that list. So you got to think about, well, how do I get on that list? Sitting back, just going to a random tournament, that's not how you get on a list, right? You be proactive in this recruiting process. You target the schools that are going to be a good fit for you, not only athletically, but academically, geographically, and you target those schools. If you're looking for a better plan to do that, you want something to follow that's proven, that's helped hundreds and hundreds of athletes, comment Nation down below, and I'll send it to you.

items.caption:
This is one of the biggest lies in recruiting:

“If you’re good enough, they’ll find you.”

For the top 1%? Yeah, for sure.

For everyone else, that mindset gets good players missed.

College coaches cannot recruit players they do not know about.

And today, part of the recruiting process is learning how to market yourself the right way.

That does not mean acting bigger than you are.

It does not mean posting fake hype.

It does not mean pretending to be perfect.

It means making it easy for the right coaches to evaluate you.

Your profile should clearly show:

Position
Grad year
Location
Top metrics
Grades
Video
Contact information

Then start posting your best qualities as an athlete.

Game video is great.

But practice/training video can work too.

Start with just showing your best qualities as a player - your best skill, and go from there.

College coaches want to see athletes.

They want to discover players.

They expect you to help them find you.

So stop worrying about what everyone else thinks.

Your goal is not to impress random people.

Your goal is to get in front of the right college coaches.

Stop waiting to be discovered.

Start positioning yourself.
items.transcript: If you're an athlete struggling to market yourself to college, coaches, whether via social media or highlight videos, one, I get it, but I want to give you a different perspective on this. As a former D1 coach, scout, and recruiter. And listen, I was there in high school struggling with this. I kind of just wanted to put my head down and work and train. But when I had this other perspective put into my head, and I wasn't an elite of elite athletes, you know that old saying of like, hey, if you're good enough, they'll find you. Man, that's only true for the top 1% of athletes. If you're a good athlete, but nobody knows about you, they're not just going to randomly find you. They will find the draft pick athletes for sure. But college coaches are expecting you to market. They want you to market yourself. That's how they find athletes. So I don't want you to going in of like, hey, what will other people think about me? What will coaches think about me? As a college scout recruiter, we want to see videos. We want to discover you. We want you to market yourself. So think about this. When you are marketing, focus on it doesn't all have to be game video. Game video can be good, but it can be just your best skills and training. Show off your best qualities as a player. That's what you want to get. Just start there. Start with your best quality as an athlete and post that and start following other coaches, other schools out there to help you because they get notified when you follow them, they get notified. And if you have a right profile, which is all of your top metrics, stats, and grades, where you're from, your position, your grad year on your profile, that is a good starting point to start marketing yourself. Good luck.

A college camp invite does NOT always mean real recruiting interest.

Some invites are personal.

Some are mass emails.

And some go out to thousands of athletes.

Before you spend the money, ask this:

“Coach, are you currently recruiting my position in my grad class?”

If they don’t respond, that’s usually your answer.

If they do, follow up with:

“Will there be an opportunity to get a true evaluation from your staff at camp or outside of camp?”

That’s how you stop guessing.

That’s how you find out if there’s real traction.

Parents, don’t chase every camp invite.

Chase the right opportunities.

Hard work matters, but it needs direction.

A good athlete can still get overlooked if the school list is wrong, the communication is unclear, or the family does not know what steps matter next.

That is why we built Recruit Nation.

Pennsylvania has some of the best universities in the country.

But if you’re a high school athlete going through the recruiting process, don’t just chase the biggest name or the biggest logo.

The goal is to find the best overall fit.

Athletically.
Academically.
Personally.
Financially.
Geographically.

Because the right school is not just the place that looks good on paper.

It’s the place where you can actually play.
Develop.
Compete.
Get a meaningful degree.
Build relationships.
And have a great college experience.

That’s what recruiting is really about.

Not just getting recruited.

Finding the school that gives you the best chance to win in your sport and in life after sports.

This is my Top 10 Universities in Pennsylvania ranking based on academics, outcomes, opportunity, and athletics.

I’m sure some people will agree…

And I’m sure some people will strongly disagree. 😂

So let’s hear it:

Which Pennsylvania school is too high?
Which one is too low?
And which school did I miss?

Drop your favorite Pennsylvania school in the comments.

Follow for more recruiting guidance to help athletes get to the next level.
items.transcript: Pennsylvania has some of the top schools in the world, and this is the top 10 universities in Pennsylvania. “Is Division 3 settling?”

No.

It’s not settling.

It’s playing college sports.

Only a small percentage of high school athletes ever get the chance to compete at the college level — whether that’s D1, D2, D3, NAIA, or JUCO.

The mistake I see families make is thinking the “highest level” automatically means the “best opportunity.”

It doesn’t.

The best opportunity is the school where your athlete can:

✅ Develop
✅ Compete
✅ Contribute
✅ Get a great education
✅ Build lifelong relationships
✅ Grow through the demands of being a college athlete

Yes, there are differences between levels.

Scholarships are different.
Competition is different.
Time commitments are different.
Exposure is different.

But the core experience?

Being part of a team.
Competing for something bigger than yourself.
Learning discipline.
Handling adversity.
Building friendships that last a lifetime.

That exists at every level.

I’ve seen athletes chase D1 so hard that they missed incredible D2, D3, JUCO, and NAIA opportunities right in front of them.

And sometimes, those “lower-level” opportunities are exactly what open the door to the best college experience — or even a chance to transfer up later.

Parents and athletes: don’t let ego make the decision.

Find the right fit.

Athletically. Academically. Socially. Financially.

Because playing college sports at any level is not settling.

It’s earned.
items.transcript: Is Division III settling? I just had a parent ask me that, and as a former D1 coach, scout, and recruiter, here's what I said. Only around 10% of high school athletes play in college at either D1, D2, D3, JUCO, or NAI. So no, it's not settling. It is a leap. There's differences and similarities at each of these levels, but chasing the wrong levels, you can miss on such great opportunities that can help propel you in your life. The differences are like scholarship, competition level, and time commitment. The similarities, being a part of a team and a brotherhood or a sisterhood that's bigger than yourself, and you're going to have those friendships the rest of your life because you guys went through the hard times. This is also about resilience and being a student-athlete. In college, there's a lot of ups and downs. It's not like being a normal student in college. There's early mornings, late evenings, getting home at 1, 2, 3 o'clock in the morning off of the bus, but it is all worth it. Don't just chase the top schools where you could play at a lower-level school like a D3 or D2, and maybe eventually transfer up if you play well enough. But going after D1s and missing great opportunities and maybe not playing at all, it's not worth it. Be open-minded to all of the levels. Good luck.

Talent matters.

But talent alone does not get you recruited.

As a former Division I coach, scout, and recruiter, I’ve watched plenty of talented athletes who had the ability…

But I also watched coaches pass on them because something was missing.

College coaches are not just asking:

“Can this athlete play?”

They’re also asking:

Can they handle coaching?

Can they respond when they fail?

Are they a good teammate?

Do they lead by how they prepare and compete?

Can they get into our school academically?

Because when a coach offers you, they’re not just betting on your talent.

They’re betting on who you are when things get hard.

Athletes, don’t just focus on being seen.

Focus on being the type of player coaches want to bring into their program.

Parents, these are the traits your athlete needs to understand early.

There is a way coaches evaluate recruiting families that most people on the outside never learn.

They are looking at how organized the outreach is. Whether the film proves fit fast. Whether the family understands the roster situation. Whether the follow up feels intentional or desperate.

Every one of those things is a signal. And coaches read those signals in seconds.

The families who crack that code early are the ones whose athletes end up in the conversations that matter. The ones who never learn it spend years wondering why nobody is calling back.

The College Recruiting Playbook hands you that code from day one. Every step. Every signal. Every part of the process coaches are actually paying attention to.
Your sport. Your year. Your division. All of it already inside.

Comment PLAN and I will send you the link right now.

#CollegeRecruiting #RecruitingAdvice #StudentAthlete #CollegeAthletes #RecruitingSystem

Most high school athletes think talking to college coaches is casual.

It’s not.

It’s an evaluation.

When a college coach starts asking questions, they’re not just making small talk…

They’re trying to figure out:

✅ Are you mature?
✅ Are you a team-first athlete?
✅ Can you communicate?
✅ Do your grades fit?
✅ Are you actually interested in their school?
✅ Do you understand what it takes at the next level?

And the fastest way to lose momentum is being unprepared.

Here are 3 questions coaches will almost always ask:

1️⃣ “How is your season going?”
Don’t start with your stats.

Start with the team.

Talk about how the team is doing, what your role has been, how you’re helping the team win, and then mention your individual performance.

Coaches want competitors. Not just stat collectors.

2️⃣ “How are your grades?”
They’re checking if you can get admitted.

Your talent matters, but if your academics don’t fit, it can stop the process fast.

3️⃣ “What do you want to study?”
You don’t need to have your whole life figured out.

But you do need to answer with maturity.

If you don’t know yet, say:

“Coach, I’m still exploring options, but I’m interested in finding something that fits my strengths and gives me a good future after sports.”

That’s a solid answer.

And don’t just answer questions…

Ask good ones too.

Two questions every athlete should have ready:

➡️ “What does a typical day look like in your program during the fall, winter, spring, and summer?”

➡️ “What are you looking for in my position in this recruiting class?”

Those questions show you’re serious, prepared, and thinking like a college athlete.

Recruiting is not just about being seen.

It’s about being ready when the opportunity comes.

Big-time facilities are impressive.

Weight rooms.
Recovery rooms.
Stadiums.
Indoor complexes.
Nutrition centers.
Player lounges.
Millions of dollars invested into helping athletes perform.

And yes… when you walk into some of these places, it can feel like a pro facility.

But athletes need to hear this:

A facility does not make you successful.

Your mindset does.
Your work ethic does.
Your consistency does.
Your teammates do.
Your coaches do.
Your willingness to show up every day and compete does.

I played at the Division 1 mid-major level (not big-time facilities), and it was one of the best experiences of my life.

Not because we had the biggest facility in the country.

But because I loved the game.
I loved my teammates.
I loved going to battle with the guys beside me.
I loved competing for something bigger than myself.

That’s what college athletics is really about.

Of course, chase big dreams.

But don’t make the mistake of thinking your dream only matters if it happens at one of those places.

There are athletes having life-changing experiences at:

D1 mid-majors
D2 schools
D3 schools
NAIA programs
JUCOs
Small colleges most people have never heard of

The goal is not just to find the biggest logo.

The goal is to find a place where you can:

Develop.
Compete.
Play.
Grow.
Build lifelong relationships.
Earn a meaningful degree.
And become the best version of yourself.

Facilities are cool.

But fit matters more.

Stay focused.
Keep working.
Know what is important.
And don’t let someone else’s version of success make you miss your own.

Follow for more recruiting guidance to help athletes get to the next level.
